name: 把 (bǎ) disposal construction
explanation: The 把 construction moves the object before the verb to emphasise what is DONE TO that object: Subject + 把 + Object + Verb + (result/complement). The verb must be transitive and must carry something extra after it (a complement, 了, a result), because 把 stresses the disposal or effect on the object.
common_mistake: English speakers use 把 with a bare verb and no result (把书看), or use it with intransitive/stative verbs, or combine it with 被 in the same clause.

name: 被 (bèi) passive construction
explanation: 被 marks the passive: Receiver + 被 + (Agent) + Verb + (extra). The subject is the thing acted UPON. The agent after 被 is optional. Like 把, the verb usually needs a complement or 了 after it.
common_mistake: Combining 被 with 把 in one clause, or leaving the verb bare with no complement.

name: 了 (le) as completed action
explanation: Verb + 了 marks that an action is completed or actualised, especially with a quantified or specific object: 我买了三本书. It is about completion of a specific event, not past tense in general.
common_mistake: Treating 了 as a past-tense marker and attaching it to every past action, including habitual or stative ones (以前我是了学生).

name: 了 (le) as change of state (sentence-final)
explanation: Sentence-final 了 signals a new situation or change of state: 我饿了 (I'm hungry now), 天黑了 (it's gotten dark). It reports that something is now different from before.
common_mistake: Omitting sentence-final 了 when reporting a new state, or confusing it with the verbal completion 了.
incorrect_example: 我今年二十岁。（when reporting a birthday just passed）

name: 过 (guò) experiential aspect
explanation: Verb + 过 says an experience has happened at least once in the past: 我去过中国 (I have been to China). It emphasises past experience, not a specific completed event.
common_mistake: Using 了 where experiential 过 is meant, or adding 过 to ongoing/present situations.
incorrect_example: 我吃了日本菜，很喜欢。（meaning: I have tried Japanese food）

name: 是……的 (shì...de) emphasis construction
explanation: For a completed action, 是……的 emphasises a circumstance — when, where, how, or with whom: 我是昨天来的 (it was yesterday that I came). 是 goes before the emphasised element; 的 comes at the end.
common_mistake: Using 了 instead of 是……的 to highlight the time/place/manner of a known past event.
incorrect_example: 你怎么来了这里？（asking by what means）

name: Measure words (量词)
explanation: Chinese requires a measure word between a number/demonstrative and a noun: Number + Measure Word + Noun. Different nouns take different measure words (本 for books, 个 for general items, 张 for flat things, 杯 for cups).
common_mistake: Dropping the measure word (三书) or defaulting to 个 for everything (三个书).

name: 在/正在 (zài/zhèngzài) progressive aspect
explanation: 在 or 正在 before the verb marks an action in progress: 我在看书 (I am reading). 呢 can be added at the end for emphasis. This describes ongoing actions, not states.
common_mistake: Applying 在……呢 to stative verbs (在知道), or confusing progressive 在 with the locational 在 (to be at).

name: 得 (de) degree/manner complement
explanation: Verb + 得 + complement describes how well or to what degree an action is done: 他跑得很快 (he runs fast). If the verb has an object, repeat the verb: 他说汉语说得很好.
common_mistake: Omitting the repeated verb when there is an object (他说汉语得很好), or confusing 得 with 的/地.

name: 的 / 得 / 地 (de) distinction
explanation: All read 'de' but differ: 的 links a modifier to a noun (我的书), 地 links an adverbial to a verb (慢慢地走), 得 links a verb to a complement of degree/result (走得慢).
common_mistake: Writing 的 for all three because they sound the same.

name: 比 (bǐ) comparison
explanation: A + 比 + B + Adjective states A is more [adj] than B: 他比我高. Do NOT use 很/非常 with the adjective; use 更 or a concrete amount (高一点儿, 高得多).
common_mistake: Inserting 很/非常 before the adjective (他比我很高), a direct calque of 'much taller'.

name: Word order: time and place before the verb
explanation: Chinese puts time-when and place adverbials BEFORE the verb, not after: Subject + Time + Place + Verb + Object. 我明天在家看书, not 我看书在家明天.
common_mistake: Following English order and putting time/place after the verb or object.

name: Duration complement (time how long)
explanation: Duration goes AFTER the verb: Verb + Duration. With an object, repeat the verb or place duration between verb and object: 我学汉语学了两年 / 我学了两年汉语.
common_mistake: Placing the duration before the verb like a time-when phrase (我两年学汉语).

name: 两 (liǎng) vs 二 (èr)
explanation: Use 两 before a measure word to mean 'two of something' (两个人); use 二 for the number itself, counting, and ordinals (第二, 二十).
common_mistake: Using 二 before a measure word (二个人).

name: 有点儿 (yǒudiǎnr) vs 一点儿 (yìdiǎnr)
explanation: 有点儿 + Adjective goes before the adjective and implies something undesirable (有点儿贵 = a bit too expensive). Adjective + 一点儿 goes after and means 'a little more' as a comparison (便宜一点儿 = a little cheaper).
common_mistake: Swapping them: 一点儿贵 or 有点儿便宜 in the wrong slot.

name: 会 / 能 / 可以 (huì/néng/kěyǐ) modals
explanation: 会 = a learned ability (会说汉语); 能 = physical capability or possibility in the moment (今天不能来); 可以 = permission (这里可以抽烟吗). They overlap but the core sense differs.
common_mistake: Using 能 for learned skills or 会 for permission.

name: 就 (jiù) vs 才 (cái)
explanation: 就 suggests something happens sooner/easily than expected (他七点就来了 = as early as 7); 才 suggests later/harder than expected (他九点才来 = not until 9). Note 就 often takes 了; 才 usually does not.
common_mistake: Adding 了 after 才, or reversing the earlier/later nuance.

name: Resultative complements (看完, 听懂)
explanation: A second verb/adjective attaches to the main verb to show the RESULT: 看完 (finish reading), 听懂 (understand by listening), 写错 (write wrongly). Negate with 没 (没看完), not 不.
common_mistake: Expressing the result separately in English style, or negating with 不 (不看完).

name: Directional complements (进来, 出去)
explanation: 来/去 and directional verbs (上下进出回过) attach to a verb to show direction relative to the speaker: 进来 (come in), 拿出去 (take out). A place object goes before 来/去: 回家来.
common_mistake: Wrong order of the place object and 来/去, or omitting the direction.

name: 因为……所以…… (yīnwèi...suǒyǐ) cause and result
explanation: Paired conjunctions are common in Chinese: 因为 (because) in the first clause, 所以 (so) in the second. Using both is natural, unlike English which uses only one.
common_mistake: Assuming that using both 'because' and 'so' is wrong, as it would be in English, and dropping 所以.
incorrect_example: 因为下雨，我没去。（acceptable, but learners often force English single-conjunction）

name: 虽然……但是…… (suīrán...dànshì) although
explanation: 虽然 (although) pairs with 但是/可是 (but) in the following clause. Both are used together in Chinese.
common_mistake: Dropping 但是 because English uses only 'although'.
correct_example: 虽然很累，但是我很开心。

name: 还是 (háishì) vs 或者 (huòzhě) — 'or'
explanation: 还是 is 'or' in questions offering a choice (你喝茶还是咖啡？); 或者 is 'or' in statements (我喝茶或者咖啡都行).
common_mistake: Using 或者 in a choice question or 还是 in a statement.

name: Verb + 了 + object + 了 (ongoing up to now)
explanation: Two 了 with a duration mean an action has been going on and continues: 我学了两年汉语了 (I have been studying Chinese for two years and still am). The sentence-final 了 adds the 'up to now, still true' sense.
common_mistake: Using one 了 and losing the 'and still continuing' meaning.
incorrect_example: 我在这儿住了三年。（meaning: and still live here）

name: 呢 (ne) follow-up question
explanation: 呢 turns a noun/pronoun into a 'what about...?' follow-up question when a topic is already established: 我很好，你呢？(I'm fine, and you?).
common_mistake: Using 吗 for a follow-up 'what about you' (你吗？), which is ungrammatical here.

name: 着 (zhe) durative / accompanying state
explanation: Verb + 着 marks a continuing state or a manner accompanying another action (V1着 + V2): 门开着 (the door is open), 他笑着说 (he said, smiling). It describes a persisting state, not a dynamic action in progress (that is 在).
common_mistake: Using 着 for a plain ongoing action where 在 is needed, or dropping 着 for an accompanying manner.

name: (快)要……了 imminent action
explanation: 要……了 / 快要……了 / 就要……了 says something is about to happen: 火车快要开了 (the train is about to leave). The final 了 is required.
common_mistake: Omitting the final 了, or using 会 for an imminent event.

name: 没……过 negating an experience
explanation: Negate experiential 过 with 没(有) while KEEPING 过: 我没去过中国 (I have never been to China).
common_mistake: Negating with 不, or dropping 过 in the negative.

name: 没 negates a completed action and drops 了
explanation: To negate a completed action, use 没(有) before the verb and DROP 了: 我没去 (I didn't go), never 我没去了.
common_mistake: Keeping 了 after 没 (没……了).

name: 吗 (ma) yes/no question particle
explanation: Add 吗 to the end of a statement to make a yes/no question: 你是学生吗？ Do not use it when the sentence already contains a question word.
common_mistake: Adding 吗 to a question that already has a question word (哪儿 / 什么 / 谁).

name: 吧 (ba) suggestion / supposition
explanation: Sentence-final 吧 softens a statement into a suggestion (我们走吧 let's go) or a supposition seeking confirmation (你是老师吧？ you're a teacher, right?).
common_mistake: Using 吗 for a suggestion or a 'right?' supposition where 吧 is natural.

name: 的 (de) attributive / relative clause
explanation: 的 links a modifier — including a whole relative clause — to the noun it describes: 我买的书 (the book I bought), 穿红衣服的人 (the person wearing red). The modifier goes before 的 and before the noun.
common_mistake: Dropping 的 in a relative clause, or following English word order.

name: 会……的 future certainty
explanation: 会 + Verb + 的 expresses a confident prediction: 别担心，他会来的 (don't worry, he'll definitely come).
common_mistake: Dropping 会 or the framing 的; using 要 for a confident prediction.

name: 想 vs 要 (to want)
explanation: 想 + Verb = would like to / feel like (softer, more polite); 要 + Verb = want to / intend to (stronger, can sound demanding). For a mild wish, prefer 想.
common_mistake: Using 要 for a polite wish where 想 is more appropriate.
incorrect_example: 老师，我要看看您的书，可以吗？
correct_example: 老师，我想看看您的书，可以吗？

name: 应该 (yīnggāi) should / ought to
explanation: 应该 + Verb = should / ought to. It is an auxiliary and goes before the main verb.
common_mistake: Placing 应该 at the end like an English tag, or omitting it.

name: 得 (děi) must / have to
explanation: 得(děi) + Verb = must / have to (colloquial). Its negative ('need not') is 不用, NOT 不得.
common_mistake: Negating with 不得 to mean 'don't have to'.

name: 可能 (kěnéng) possibly / maybe
explanation: 可能 = maybe / possibly; as an adverb it goes before the verb: 他可能来 (he might come).
common_mistake: Placing 可能 at the end of the sentence like English 'maybe'.

name: A-not-A question
explanation: Repeat the verb/adjective in affirmative-negative form for a neutral yes/no question: 你去不去？ 好不好？ Do NOT add 吗.
common_mistake: Combining an A-not-A question with 吗.

name: Question words stay in place (in-situ)
explanation: A question word (什么/谁/哪儿/怎么) occupies the SAME slot the answer would; the sentence is not reordered as in English: 你想吃什么？
common_mistake: Fronting the question word English-style.

name: 几 vs 多少 (how many)
explanation: 几 asks about small numbers and takes a measure word (几个人); 多少 asks about larger or unknown quantities and needs no measure word (多少钱).
common_mistake: Using 几 for large amounts, or inserting a measure word that is not needed after 多少.

name: 怎么样 (zěnmeyàng) how / how about
explanation: 怎么样 asks for an assessment ('how is it?') or makes a suggestion ('how about?'): 天气怎么样？ 这个怎么样？ 怎么 alone asks manner/reason ('how / why').
common_mistake: Using 怎么 where 怎么样 (how about / how is it) is meant.
correct_example: 这个周末去爬山，怎么样？

name: A 没有 B (那么) + adj — not as ... as
explanation: A + 没有 + B + (那么/这么) + Adjective = A is not as [adj] as B: 我没有他高. This, not 不比, is the normal way to say 'not as ... as'.
common_mistake: Using 不比 (which means 'not necessarily more than') to mean 'not as ... as'.

name: A 跟/和 B 一样 — the same as
explanation: A + 跟/和 + B + 一样 (+ Adjective) states A is the same as B, or equally [adj]: 我的书跟你的一样 / 他跟我一样高.
common_mistake: Dropping 一样, or omitting the connective 跟/和.

name: 越来越 (yuèláiyuè) more and more
explanation: 越来越 + Adjective/Verb = more and more: 天气越来越冷 (the weather is getting colder and colder).
common_mistake: Calquing English with 更来更 or repeated 比.

name: 越 A 越 B — the more ... the more
explanation: 越 + Verb1 + 越 + Verb2/Adj = the more A, the more B: 雨越下越大 / 你越说越快.
common_mistake: Using 更 twice, or English 'the more ... the more' word order.

name: 比 + adj + amount of difference
explanation: With 比, the amount of difference goes AFTER the adjective: 他比我高一点儿 / 高得多 / 高两公分.
common_mistake: Placing the amount before the adjective, or using 很/非常.

name: 如果……就…… (if ... then)
explanation: 如果 (+的话) in the condition clause pairs with 就 in the result clause: 如果你有时间，就来我家.
common_mistake: Dropping 就 in the result clause.
correct_example: 如果你有时间，就来我家。

name: 只要……就…… (as long as)
explanation: 只要 states a sufficient condition and pairs with 就: 只要努力，就能成功 (as long as you work hard, you'll succeed).
common_mistake: Pairing 只要 with 才 (that belongs to 只有).

name: 只有……才…… (only if)
explanation: 只有 states a necessary condition and pairs with 才: 只有努力，才能成功 (only by working hard can you succeed).
common_mistake: Pairing 只有 with 就 (that belongs to 只要).

name: 不但……而且…… (not only ... but also)
explanation: 不但 in the first clause pairs with 而且 (often + 也/还) in the second: 他不但会说英语，而且会说法语.
common_mistake: Dropping 而且/也/还 in the second clause.
incorrect_example: 他不但会说英语，会说法语。
correct_example: 他不但会说英语，而且会说法语。

name: 一……就…… (as soon as)
explanation: 一 + Event1 + 就 + Event2 = as soon as A, then B: 我一到家就给你打电话.
common_mistake: Using 然后 for the immediate second event, or dropping 就.
incorrect_example: 我一到家，然后给你打电话。
correct_example: 我一到家就给你打电话。

name: 除了……以外 (besides / except)
explanation: 除了……(以外) pairs with 都 (except: everyone else) or 还/也 (besides: in addition): 除了他以外，我们都去了 / 除了英语，我还会说中文.
common_mistake: Dropping the paired 都/还/也 in the main clause.
correct_example: 除了他以外，我们都去了。

name: 又……又…… (both ... and)
explanation: 又 + Adj1 + 又 + Adj2 links two simultaneous qualities: 这个菜又便宜又好吃.
common_mistake: Using 和 to join two predicate adjectives.

name: 一边……一边…… (doing two things at once)
explanation: 一边 + V1 + 一边 + V2 = do two actions simultaneously: 他一边吃饭一边看电视.
common_mistake: Using 和 to join two simultaneous actions.
correct_example: 他一边吃饭一边看电视。

name: 先……然后…… (first ... then)
explanation: 先 marks the first action, 然后(再) the next: 我们先吃饭，然后看电影.
common_mistake: Reversing 先/然后 or omitting 先.
incorrect_example: 我们然后看电影先吃饭。
correct_example: 我们先吃饭，然后看电影。

name: 不是……而是…… (not A but B)
explanation: 不是 A 而是 B corrects a wrong assumption: 这不是茶，而是咖啡.
common_mistake: Using 但是 instead of 而是 for a correction.

name: 无论/不管……都…… (no matter)
explanation: 无论/不管 + a choice/question word pairs with 都/也 in the main clause: 不管天气怎么样，我都去.
common_mistake: Dropping 都/也, or using 虽然.
correct_example: 不管天气怎么样，我都去。

name: ……的时候 (when / while)
explanation: A time clause is closed by 的时候 at its END, before the main clause: 我小的时候，住在北京 (when I was young, I lived in Beijing).
common_mistake: Using an English-style front 'when' without 的时候.

name: 以前 / 以后 clause order
explanation: 以前 (before) and 以后 (after) come AFTER the event they refer to: 下课以后 (after class), 睡觉以前 (before sleeping).
common_mistake: Following English order and putting 以前/以后 first.

name: Potential complement 得/不 (听得懂 / 听不懂)
explanation: Insert 得 (can) or 不 (cannot) between a verb and its result complement for ability/possibility: 听得懂 (can understand) / 看不清楚 (cannot see clearly).
common_mistake: Using 不 + Verb + complement (不看清楚) instead of the infixed 看不清楚.
incorrect_example: 这个字太小，我不看清楚。
correct_example: 这个字太小，我看不清楚。

name: 到 as a resultative (找到 / 看到)
explanation: 到 attaches to a verb to mark that its goal was attained: 找到 (find), 看到 (catch sight of), 买到 (manage to buy).
common_mistake: Omitting 到, so successful attainment is not expressed.

name: 见 for involuntary perception (看见 / 听见)
explanation: 见 attaches to perception verbs to mark the result of perceiving: 看见 (see), 听见 (hear) — the perception actually registered.
common_mistake: Using bare 看/听 when the perceptual result is meant.
incorrect_example: 我听了一个奇怪的声音。
correct_example: 我听见了一个奇怪的声音。

name: Verb + 给 + recipient (送给 / 还给)
explanation: 给 after certain verbs marks transfer to a recipient: 送给他一本书 (give him a book), 还给我 (return to me).
common_mistake: Omitting 给 or misordering the recipient.

name: 极了 / 死了 extreme degree
explanation: Adjective + 极了/死了/坏了 = extremely (colloquial), and takes 了: 好极了 (great), 累死了 (dead tired).
common_mistake: Combining with 很, or omitting 了.

name: 好 as a resultative (做好 / 准备好)
explanation: 好 as a complement marks that an action is done properly / is ready: 做好了 (done), 准备好 (ready), 坐好 (sit properly).
common_mistake: Omitting 好 when 'ready / done properly' is meant.

name: 起来 — inception / 'seem' / recall
explanation: 起来 extends beyond 'up': it marks the start of an action (下起雨来 it started raining), a judgement (看起来 it looks..., 说起来 speaking of...), or recall (想起来 to recall).
common_mistake: Reading 起来 only literally as 'up' and omitting it for these senses.
correct_example: 我突然想起来那个词了。

name: 下去 — continue an action
explanation: Verb + 下去 = keep doing / continue: 说下去 (go on speaking), 坚持下去 (keep it up).
common_mistake: Using 下来 for 'continue', or 继续 awkwardly without the complement.
incorrect_example: 老师让我们继续读下来。

name: 从……到…… (from ... to)
explanation: 从 A 到 B spans time or space: 我从九点到五点工作 / 从北京到上海.
common_mistake: Using 到 alone, or following English word order with the times after the verb.

name: 离 (lí) distance from
explanation: A + 离 + B + 远/近 states the distance between two points: 我家离学校很近.
common_mistake: Using 从 for a static distance.

name: 对 (duì) toward / regarding
explanation: 对 + object introduces the target of an attitude or effect: 他对我很好 (he's good to me), 运动对身体好 (exercise is good for the body).
common_mistake: Using 给 or 跟 where 对 (toward/regarding) is needed.

name: 给 (gěi) + recipient + verb (for / to)
explanation: 给 + recipient + Verb marks who an action is done for/to: 我给你打电话 (I'll call you), 给妈妈做饭.
common_mistake: Omitting 给, or following English order (verb before recipient) where the coverb is expected.

name: 跟/和 (gēn/hé) + person (with)
explanation: 跟/和 + person + Verb means doing something with someone: 我跟朋友去看电影.
common_mistake: Putting 'with ...' at the end English-style, or using 用.

name: 往/向 (wǎng/xiàng) toward a direction
explanation: 往/向 + direction + Verb points the action a way: 往前走 (walk forward), 向右拐 (turn right).
common_mistake: Using 到/去 for a bare direction of motion.

name: 为了 (wèile) in order to
explanation: 为了 + purpose, then the main clause: 为了找到好工作，我在学中文.
common_mistake: Placing the purpose at the end, or using 因为 (because) for a purpose.
incorrect_example: 我学中文因为找到好工作。
correct_example: 为了找到好工作，我在学中文。

name: 用 (yòng) + tool + verb (by means of)
explanation: 用 + instrument + Verb says what is used to do something: 用筷子吃饭 (eat with chopsticks), 用中文写.
common_mistake: Following English 'with ...' order and putting the tool after the verb.

name: 在 + place + verb (do at a location)
explanation: The location coverb 在 + place goes BEFORE the verb: 我在图书馆学习.
common_mistake: Following English order and putting the place after the verb.

name: 有 (yǒu) existential — there is/are
explanation: Place + 有 + Noun introduces the existence of something: 桌子上有一本书 (there's a book on the table).
common_mistake: Using 是 or 在 to introduce a newly-mentioned existing thing.

name: 是 (shì) identity — no 是 before an adjective
explanation: 是 equates two nouns (A 是 B): 他是老师. It is NOT used before an adjective predicate — use 很 there instead.
common_mistake: Inserting 是 before an adjective (他是高 / 他是很忙).

name: 在 (zài) to be located at
explanation: A specific/known thing + 在 + Place states where it is: 我的手机在桌子上 / 爸爸在家.
common_mistake: Using 有 or 是 for the location of a specific known thing.

name: Number + 多 approximation
explanation: 多 after a round number means '-odd / more than': 三十多岁 (thirty-odd), 一百多块.
common_mistake: Putting 多 before the number, or omitting it for 'more than'.

name: 半 (bàn) half
explanation: 半 before a measure word = half (半个小时); after Number+MW = 'and a half' (一个半小时).
common_mistake: Putting the number after 半, or wrong slot for 'and a half'.

name: 第 (dì) ordinal numbers
explanation: 第 + Number (+ Measure Word) forms an ordinal: 第一次 (the first time), 第三个.
common_mistake: Omitting 第 for an ordinal (using a bare cardinal).

name: 每 (měi) + measure word (every)
explanation: 每 + MW + Noun = every; the main clause usually takes 都: 每个学生都喜欢老师.
common_mistake: Omitting the measure word after 每, or dropping 都.

name: 些 / 一些 / 这些 (some, plural)
explanation: 些 marks an unspecified plural amount: 一些书, 这些人. It replaces number+MW and takes no measure word of its own.
common_mistake: Adding a measure word after 些 (一些个) or a number before it.

name: Reduplicated measure word (天天 / 个个) — every one
explanation: Reduplicating a measure word means 'every single one': 天天 (every day), 个个 (every one), 条条.
common_mistake: Combining with 每 (每天天) or misforming the reduplication.

name: Dates: year–month–day order
explanation: Dates run big-to-small: 年 + 月 + 日/号: 2026年7月13号.
common_mistake: Using English small-to-big order.

name: Telling the clock (点 / 分 / 半 / 刻)
explanation: Clock time uses 点 (o'clock), 分 (minute), 半 (half past), 刻 (quarter): 两点半 / 三点一刻. Use 两 (not 二) for 2 o'clock.
common_mistake: Using 二 for 2 o'clock, or 小时 (duration) for clock time.

name: Spoken money (块 / 毛 / 分)
explanation: In speech, money uses 块 (=元), 毛 (=角), 分, big unit first: 三块五 (3.5 yuan), 十块二毛.
common_mistake: Using written 元/角 in speech, or reversing the unit order.

name: Adjective predicate takes 很, not 是
explanation: An adjective predicate needs a degree adverb (usually a bland 很) and NO 是: 他很高. A bare adjective (他高) implies comparison/contrast.
common_mistake: Using 是 before the adjective, or leaving a neutral statement with a bare adjective.

name: 也 (yě) also — before the verb
explanation: 也 (also/too) goes before the verb or adjective, never at the end: 我也去.
common_mistake: Placing 也 at the end like English 'too'.

name: 都 (dōu) all/both — before the verb, scope to its left
explanation: 都 goes before the verb and refers to something already mentioned to its LEFT: 我们都是学生.
common_mistake: Placing 都 before the subject it quantifies, or after the verb.

name: 还 (hái) still / in addition — before the verb
explanation: 还 before the verb means still / yet / in addition: 他还在睡觉 (still sleeping), 我还要一个 (I want one more).
common_mistake: Using 又/再 for 'still', or placing 还 at the end.

name: 再 vs 又 (again: future vs past)
explanation: 再 = again for a future/unrealised repetition (再来一次); 又 = again for a past/realised one (他又迟到了).
common_mistake: Swapping them — 再 for a completed repetition or 又 for a future one.

name: 只 (zhǐ) only — before the verb
explanation: 只 (only) goes before the verb: 我只有十块钱 / 他只喜欢你.
common_mistake: Placing 只 after the verb, or using 就 for 'only'.

name: 太……了 (too / so)
explanation: 太 + Adjective + 了 = too / so (excess or exclamation): 太贵了 / 太好了.
common_mistake: Omitting 了 after 太, or replacing 太 with 很.

name: 常常 / 经常 frequency — before the verb
explanation: Frequency adverbs (常常/经常/有时候) go before the verb: 我常常去那儿.
common_mistake: Placing the frequency adverb after the verb or at the end.

name: Verb reduplication (看看 / 休息休息)
explanation: Reduplicating a verb softens it to 'a little / give it a try': one-syllable AA (看看) or A一A (看一看); two-syllable ABAB (休息休息), NOT AABB.
common_mistake: Reduplicating a two-syllable verb as AABB (休休息息).

name: Verb + 一下 (do briefly / once)
explanation: Verb + 一下 softens an action to 'briefly / just once', like reduplication: 看一下, 等一下.
common_mistake: Placing 一下 before the verb.

name: 来/去 + verb phrase (come/go to do)
explanation: 来/去 + (place) + Verb phrase expresses the purpose of coming or going: 我去商店买东西 (I'm going to the shop to buy things).
common_mistake: Inserting 为了 / an English 'to' marker between the motion and the purpose verb.
